subsystem: PNM/PPM (portable pixmap) image-file header parser. ppm_load_read_header() reads and validates the ASCII header of a Netpbm P2/P5 image from a stdio FILE* and populates a pnm_struct (type, width, height, bpc bytes-per-channel, numsamples) that later drives a pixel-buffer allocation. This is the classic GIMP/GdkPixbuf-style PNM loader front-end.
provenance: Identified by the GLib type vocabulary (gchar, gint, guchar, gushort, gsize via G_MAXSIZE), GLib idioms (g_warning, G_STRLOC, TRUE/FALSE), the Netpbm magic-byte logic (ASCII 'P' plus a subtype giving P2 ASCII / P5 raw pixmaps), the pnm_struct fields (type/width/height/bpc/numsamples), and the file banner 'corpus/train/train_0255_cwe189.c' plus the in-source tag 'primevul_c_validation_0326_fixed'. This is a PNM image-import routine; the CWE-189 tag flags it as a numeric-errors sample.
trust_boundary: The FILE* fp is an attacker-controlled image file: a user opens or previews an untrusted .ppm/.pnm, or the app decodes one received over the network or embedded in a document. Every byte read via fgets() into header[] is hostile: the magic bytes, the comment lines, and — most importantly — the ASCII width/height/maxval tokens that strtol() turns into the integers width, height, maxval that ultimately size a heap allocation. Nothing upstream of this function constrains those numbers.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes an open stdio stream fp (the untrusted image file) and a pointer img to a pnm_struct it will fill in. Local state: ptr is a scratch pointer used as the strtol() end-pointer for tokenizing numbers; header is a fixed-size on-stack line buffer whose size is the macro MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW (value not shown); maxval is a signed int that will hold the parsed maximum sample value. header is declared but NOT initialized, so its contents are indeterminate until a successful fgets() writes into it.

It reads the first line (up to MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW-1 bytes) into header, then checks the two-byte Netpbm magic: header[0] must equal ASCII_P (the character 'P') and header[1] must be either PIXMAP_ASCII (the '2' subtype, ASCII pixmap) or PIXMAP_RAW (the '5' subtype, raw pixmap). If the magic does not match it warns and returns FALSE. The return value of fgets is discarded, so header[0]/header[1] are read even when the read failed.
The validated subtype byte is stored into img->type so later pixel-reading code knows whether the payload is ASCII ('2') or raw binary ('5').

Netpbm headers may contain '#' comment lines. The code reads the next line and, while it begins with '#', keeps reading further lines to skip comments. When the loop exits, header is expected to hold the first non-comment line (the width/height line). Again none of the fgets return values are checked, so the loop's continuation condition depends entirely on the buffer contents being refreshed each iteration.

errno is cleared, then strtol parses the leading base-10 integer from header as the image width, leaving ptr pointing just past the digits. If strtol set errno (i.e. ERANGE overflow of long) it warns and returns FALSE; otherwise, if the parsed width is negative it also rejects. So width is validated for both range-overflow and negativity.

The height is parsed from ptr (the remainder of the same line, after the width digits), again advancing ptr. The errno branch guards against strtol overflow. The negativity branch, however, re-tests img->width (NOT img->height) even though its warning text says 'height is negative' — a copy/paste defect: the height value is never actually checked for being negative here. Note errno is not re-zeroed before this strtol; it is only 0 because the width path succeeded without setting it.

The next line is read and parsed as maxval, the maximum sample value. Only exactly 255 (8-bit samples) or 65535 (16-bit samples) are accepted; anything else is rejected. This whitelist tightly constrains maxval, so a bogus/overflowing maxval cannot slip through even though errno is not checked here.

Bytes-per-channel is derived from maxval: 1 (sizeof guchar) for 8-bit, 2 (sizeof gushort) for 16-bit. The default arm is unreachable given the preceding whitelist and only emits a warning (it does not return), so img->bpc would be left unset there — but the whitelist guarantees the default is never taken.

This is the overflow guard that the '_fixed' variant added. It rejects zero width or height, and rejects any dimensions where width*height*CHANNEL_COUNT*bpc would exceed G_MAXSIZE, by checking G_MAXSIZE / width / height / CHANNEL_COUNT < bpc (a division-based overflow-safe formulation). If it passes, numsamples = width * height * CHANNEL_COUNT is computed and the function returns TRUE. numsamples * bpc is what the caller later allocates. The %ld format specifiers imply img->width/img->height are signed long (glong).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The comment-skip loop never checks the fgets return value. When fgets hits EOF or a read error it returns NULL and leaves header UNCHANGED. If the file's comment section runs to EOF (e.g. a valid magic line followed by a '#'-prefixed line with no more data), the loop enters with header[0]=='#', the fgets at the bottom returns NULL without touching header, header[0] stays '#', and the loop spins forever. An attacker crafts such a file to hang the importing process — a denial of service (CWE-835 infinite loop) reachable from an untrusted image. The invariant: every fgets must be checked for NULL and the loop must terminate on EOF.

The first fgets return is also ignored. On an empty file, a zero-length read, or a stream error, fgets returns NULL and header is never written, so header[0]/header[1] read INDETERMINATE stack bytes (CWE-457 use of uninitialized memory; CWE-252 unchecked return). Two consequences: (1) the magic check is performed on garbage and could accidentally match, and (2) subsequent strtol calls operate on a buffer that fgets may not have NUL-terminated. The same unchecked pattern repeats at lines 25, 28, and 57. Invariant: reject immediately if fgets returns NULL before touching header.

The negativity check at line 51 tests img->width again instead of img->height, so a negative HEIGHT is never rejected by this gate. The parser therefore admits height < 0. Downstream, img->numsamples = img->width * img->height * CHANNEL_COUNT would be negative/nonsensical, corrupting the later allocation size. In this particular file the overflow guard on line 83 happens to also block negatives because the signed glong height is converted to a huge unsigned gsize in the division, making G_MAXSIZE/width/height/CHANNEL_COUNT round to 0 and trip the '< img->bpc' test — but that is incidental, not the intended defense. Relying on unsigned wraparound in the guard instead of the explicit check is fragile: any refactor of the guard, a different integer width for the dimension fields, or a caller that reuses img before the guard, re-exposes a signed integer error (CWE-190/CWE-682) feeding an allocation. The invariant the author intended — reject negative height — is not upheld.

The guard bounds the product width*height*CHANNEL_COUNT*bpc against G_MAXSIZE (a gsize), but img->numsamples is then assigned width*height*CHANNEL_COUNT. If numsamples' declared type is narrower than gsize (e.g. gint), the multiplication can wrap in signed int arithmetic BEFORE the wider-width guard would notice, since the guard divides in gsize while the assignment multiplies in the narrower type — a truncation/overflow (CWE-190) that yields a small numsamples the caller then multiplies by bpc and under-allocates against, enabling a heap overflow when pixels are read. Also, because width/height are signed and the guard divides an unsigned G_MAXSIZE by them, mixed signed/unsigned division makes the guard's behavior depend on implicit conversions rather than explicit positivity checks. The invariant: validate 0 < width, 0 < height explicitly, and perform the whole size computation in the same unsigned type used for allocation.

errno is only zeroed once, at line 32 before the width parse; it is not reset before the height strtol (line 45) nor before the maxval strtol (line 58). It is 0 on entry to the height check only as a side effect of the width parse not having failed. This is fragile errno hygiene (CWE-252/CWE-754): if any intervening library call (or a future edit) set errno, the height/maxval overflow checks would misfire. Additionally, height is parsed from ptr, the leftover of the width line, so a well-formed PNM that places width and height on SEPARATE whitespace-delimited lines is mis-parsed (height becomes 0 and is rejected) — a correctness/robustness gap. Invariant: clear errno immediately before each strtol and tokenize across newlines per the Netpbm grammar.

header is bounded by MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW and every read uses fgets(header, MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW, fp), so there is no buffer OVERFLOW here (fgets caps the copy). The residual risk is truncation: if MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW is small, a header line longer than the buffer is split, so a numeric token (or the magic pair) can straddle two fgets calls and be mis-parsed — the second fgets in the width/height flow would then read the tail of a truncated line. This is a parsing-correctness weakness (CWE-20), not memory corruption, but it can cause dimensions to be read from the wrong bytes. Confirm MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW is large enough to hold any legal header line.

summary: An untrusted-image PNM/PPM header parser with one clear DoS bug and one clear validation bug: unchecked fgets makes the comment-skip loop spin forever on a truncated/EOF comment section (CWE-835), and the height negativity check erroneously re-tests width (line 51), leaving negative height unvalidated. The added division-based overflow guard blocks the classic dimension-overflow heap corruption for positive inputs and incidentally masks the negative-height bug via unsigned conversion, but the safety of the size computation still hinges on numsamples' unshown type and on fragile errno/return-value handling.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point consuming an attacker-controlled FILE*; drives all downstream parsing.
Fixed-size line buffer; no overflow (fgets bounds it) but truncation/uninitialized-read risk.
Undisplayed buffer-size macro; if small, header lines are truncated and tokens mis-parsed.
Whitelisted to exactly 255 or 65535 before use; cannot carry a hostile value forward.
Return value never checked: uninitialized/stale buffer reads and non-termination on EOF.
Correct two-byte Netpbm magic gate; only weak because it runs on a possibly-unwritten buffer (covered by the fgets finding).
Stores an already-validated subtype byte; no separate contract.
Loop continuation depends on unchecked fgets; infinite loop on EOF (CWE-835).
Width is validated for both strtol ERANGE and negativity; correct.
Parsed from same-line remainder; errno not re-zeroed; feeds the buggy negativity check.
Copy/paste bug: re-checks width, so negative height is never rejected (CWE-190/682/20).
bpc is 1 or 2 from the whitelist; default arm unreachable.
Guards the size product but mixes signed dimensions with unsigned gsize and may not match numsamples' type.
Allocation-driving product; may overflow in a narrower type than the guard divides in (CWE-190 latent).
Constants used only inside the overflow guard; carry no independent contract.
Set solely from the maxval whitelist to 1 or 2; bounded.

findings.title: Infinite loop in comment-skip on unchecked fgets at EOF
findings.bug_class: Denial of service (unbounded loop on unchecked I/O return)
findings.invariant: Each fgets return must be checked for NULL, and the comment loop must terminate when the stream reaches EOF/error.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted image whose comment section ('#...') is not followed by any further readable data makes the bottom fgets return NULL and leave header unchanged with header[0]=='#'; the while condition stays true forever, hanging the process on an untrusted file.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm fgets return is not checked and that no other loop-exit exists on EOF; verify the process actually spins on a comment-then-EOF input.

findings.title: Unchecked fgets return -> uninitialized/stale header buffer
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialized memory / unchecked return value
findings.invariant: Return FALSE immediately if fgets returns NULL, before reading header[] or handing it to strtol.
findings.failure_mode: On an empty file, short read, or stream error, fgets returns NULL and never writes header; the magic test (header[0]/header[1]) and later strtol then read indeterminate, possibly non-NUL-terminated stack bytes, giving nondeterministic acceptance/parsing of a nominally invalid file (repeats at lines 25, 28, 57).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm none of the four fgets calls check their result and that header is not pre-zeroed.

findings.title: Negative height accepted: gate re-checks width instead of height
findings.bug_class: Signed integer / improper-input validation (wrong variable checked)
findings.invariant: A parsed height must be rejected when negative; the check at line 51 must test img->height, not img->width.
findings.failure_mode: strtol yields a negative height (e.g. '-1' in the header) and the check at line 51 tests width instead, so it passes. numsamples = width*height*CHANNEL_COUNT then becomes negative/garbage feeding an allocation. In this file the line-83 guard incidentally blocks it because the signed height converts to a huge gsize and forces the '< bpc' rejection, but that masking is fragile and disappears under any type or guard change.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the height gate checks img->width (line 51) and trace whether the overflow guard actually rejects negative height for the real declared types of width/height/numsamples.

findings.title: numsamples product may overflow a narrower type than the guard divides in
findings.bug_class: Integer overflow / truncation in size computation
findings.invariant: The size computation must be done in the SAME unsigned type used to allocate, and width/height positivity must be checked explicitly rather than via signed/unsigned division side effects.
findings.failure_mode: The guard divides an unsigned G_MAXSIZE by signed width/height, but the assignment multiplies in numsamples' declared type; if that type is narrower (e.g. gint) the product can wrap to a small value that passes the wider-width guard, so the caller under-allocates numsamples*bpc bytes and overflows the pixel buffer when reading the payload.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared types of img->width, img->height, and img->numsamples and whether the multiply is performed in gsize; verify the allocation uses numsamples*bpc.

findings.title: Fragile errno handling around height/maxval strtol
findings.bug_class: Improper check of function return value (errno not reset per call)
findings.invariant: errno must be set to 0 immediately before every strtol whose ERANGE result is inspected.
findings.failure_mode: errno is cleared only once at line 32; the height (line 45) and maxval (line 58) strtol calls rely on errno still being 0 from the width path. Any intervening errno-setting call, or a future edit, makes the height overflow check read a stale errno and either false-reject or miss a range error.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no errno=0 precedes the height and maxval strtol calls and evaluate whether a stale errno is reachable.

findings.title: Header line truncation from small MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW
findings.invariant: MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW must exceed the longest legal Netpbm header line so no magic/numeric token is split across fgets calls.
findings.failure_mode: If MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW is small, a long header line is split so a subsequent fgets reads a token fragment; width/height/maxval can then be parsed from the wrong bytes. No memory corruption occurs (fgets bounds the copy), but dimensions may be mis-read.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the numeric value of MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW and that it comfortably exceeds any valid header line length.

audit_checklist: Check every fgets return for NULL and bail out before touching header[]; guarantee the comment loop terminates at EOF (fixes the infinite-loop DoS).
Fix the height negativity gate at line 51 to test img->height, and add explicit 0 < width && 0 < height checks that do not rely on unsigned division side effects.
Confirm the declared types of img->width, img->height, img->numsamples and perform width*height*CHANNEL_COUNT*bpc entirely in the unsigned gsize type used by the allocator.
Set errno = 0 immediately before each strtol (width already done; add for height and maxval) and validate all parsed tokens are fully consumed.
Verify MAX_CHARS_IN_ROW is large enough that no legal header line (magic, comment, or numeric) is truncated across reads.
Trace the caller's allocation of numsamples*bpc and its pixel-read loop to confirm the guard here actually protects it.
